original_abstract: Receiving a cancer diagnosis necessitates making high-stakes, complex decisions regarding treatment pathways. In this chapter, we detail an interdisciplinary research initiative designed to automatically generate personalized descriptions of treatment options to assist patients in this process. Our approach utilizes two extensive databases curated by the Netherlands Comprehensive Cancer Organisation (IKNL): the Netherlands Cancer Registry and the PROFILES dataset. By integrating these sources, we were able to extract tailored clinical data and outcomes for various cancer types. We then augmented these statistics with personalized context—presented through both verbal descriptors and patient narratives—to better facilitate shared decision-making. We conclude by evaluating the strengths and limitations of our methodology, discussing its generalizability to other health domains, and offering broader reflections on the project’s outcomes.
humanized_abstract: When patients receive a cancer diagnosis, they are faced with making a series of critical and potentially difficult decisions regarding their treatment. This chapter reports on an interdisciplinary effort to generate human readable, individualised listings of the characteristics of treatment options that may aid patients in these decisions. Using the cancer registries (Netherlands Cancer Registry) and profiles datasets (PROFILES) of the Netherlands Comprehensive Cancer Organisation (IKNL), we gathered individualised clinical statistics per type of cancer for multiple tumour sites and potentially relevant non-cancer variables. These data were combined with patient-generated descriptions and quotations per tumour site to create an informative base to support patients and their doctors during the process of sharing information when deciding which treatment to undergo. We reflect on our research and the contribution it makes, both in terms of understanding the impact of our research and its generalisability to other healthcare contexts and patient health decisions.
